originalTitle: Morning Mail: Oil hits US$100 as Trump threatens Iran, Musk muses on ‘meaningless’ money, Commonwealth Games begin
summary: More economic uncertainty as president signals another escalation in the Middle East; trillionaire says robots could do away with money
content: Morning everyone. The cost of living issues clouding Labor’s national conference could become more acute after oil soared over US$100 a barrel again overnight and Donald Trump threatened “a massive attack” on Iran. We have full reports on the worsening situation in the Middle East. We also look at how Pauline Hanson is mining the US president’s playbook after a week of controversy, Elon Musk says money might become meaningless and speculates that the universe is a computer simulation created by aliens, and – speaking of which – the Commonwealth Games have opened in Glasgow with King Charles stepping out of Doctor Who’s Tardis. Australia The Games begin | The Commonwealth Games have opened in Glasgow in a scaled down event forced on the organisers by the late withdrawal of Victoria. Follow the opening ceremony live, while the Commonwealth’s fastest man on two wheels, Matt Richardson, has brushed off the impact of his controversial switch to England as having “zero effect” on him. Labor ‘allergic’ | Outspoken Labor backbencher Ed Husic claims the party has become “allergic” to dissent and dispute, warning the unity on show at its tightly controlled national conference could backfire. Bird song | Seven-year-old Miles Phillips’ song about the eastern rosella has gone viral and been compared to the Strokes, the B-52s and Taylor Swift. He tells Rafqa Touma how he wrote it. Origin hack | Origin Energy customers’ addresses, phone numbers and partial bank account data have been accessed in a hack, the company has confirmed. Poles apart | A Northern Territory minister has been criticised for scientifically inaccurate claims that groundwater in the territory needs to be used to prevent the Earth’s magnetic poles from flipping. World Musk musings | Elon Musk has admitted he became too consumed by politics during his volatile alliance with Donald Trump and his brief appointment as the president’s financial axeman. In an interview with the Economist, he also said robots would usher in an era of “amazing abundance” that would make money meaningless. ‘Massive attack’ | The US president, Donald Trump, has said he is close to deciding whether to launch “a massive attack” against Iran, on a scale larger than strikes already witnessed during the months-long conflict with Tehran. The oil price has climbed back above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months helped by a Houthi blockade on Saudi shipments out of the strait of Bab al-Mandab. Dubai is offering to pay family and friends of residents to visit in an effort to revive tourist numbers. Follow developments here. Al-Aqsa ‘stormed’ | Israel’s far-right minister of national security, Itamar Ben-Gvir, joined thousands of Jewish extremists who stormed Jerusalem’s most sensitive holy site overnight. French flee | More than 20,000 people, including thousands of tourists, have been evacuated as a “particularly intense” wildfire advances on the Atlantic coast of south-west France. Parkinson’s clue | Long-term exposure to road traffic noise is associated with a higher risk of Parkinson’s disease, the biggest study of its kind has found. Full Story Newsroom edition: An El Niño summer looms – we need to talk about climate change Jo Tovey is joined by Mike Ticher and assistant climate editor Donna Lu to discuss whether Australia is being too complacent about the changing climate. In-depth Pauline Hanson has hit out at the media for taking her remarks “out of context” after she was criticised for describing paid leave for those experiencing family and domestic violence as “ridiculous”. Blaming the messenger is straight from the Trump playbook, Josh Butler argues. Not the news The fearsome reputation of freezing Canberra winters had Brigid Delaney worried when she moved to the capital. An electric blanket would be essential but how to avoid sleeping with “malevolent, dangerous energy”? The answer, it seems, is flannel. Sport AFL | The former Carlton co-captain Sam Docherty talks to Jonathan Horn about cancer and the other hardships that took him to “rock bottom” and the “obsession” that helped him carve out a 13-year AFL career. Cycling | Richard Carapaz won stage 18 of the Tour de France after a breakaway group of six riders but uncertainty surrounds the health of race leader Tadej Pogacar, whose team has been swept by a virus since Monday’s rest day. World Cup | Norway’s football federation is considering a complaint to Fifa ethics investigators next month over Donald Trump’s role in clearing Folarin Balogun from a mandatory one-game ban at the World Cup. Media roundup A Geelong healthcare company has been thrust into the spotlight after Tour de France leader Tadej Pogacar wore its branded nasal strips, the Advertiser reveals. The Age reports on the imminent arrival of a new long-distance Qantas plane. The Australian features a speech by Liberal James Paterson in which he warns against the perils of populism. What’s happening today Society | ABS release data on fertility rates by age cohort. Courts | Kyle and Jackie O interlocutory hearing in Sydney. Melbourne | Application for temporary stay in retrial of Greg Lynn at Victorian supreme court. Sign up Sign up for the Breaking News Australia email If you would like to receive this Morning Mail update to your email inbox every weekday, sign up here, or finish your day with our Afternoon Update newsletter. You can follow the latest in US politics by signing up for This Week in Trumpland. Brain teaser And finally, here are the Guardian’s crosswords to keep you entertained throughout the day. Until tomorrow. Quick crossword Cryptic crossword Contact us If you have a story tip or technical issue viewing this newsletter, please reply to this email. If you are a Guardian supporter and need assistance with regards to contributions and/or digital subscriptions, please email customer.help@guardian.co.uk
chineseTitle: 早间邮报：特朗普威胁伊朗，油价触及 100 美元，马斯克沉思“毫无意义”的钱，英联邦运动会开始
chineseSummary: 总统上台后经济不确定性增加，预示着中东局势再次升级；亿万富翁称机器人可以取代金钱
chineseContent: 大家早上好。在油价隔夜再次飙升至每桶 100 美元以上且唐纳德·特朗普威胁对伊朗进行“大规模攻击”之后，困扰工党全国会议的生活成本问题可能会变得更加严重。我们有关于中东局势恶化的完整报告。我们还看看宝琳·汉森 (Pauline Hanson) 在经历一周的争议后如何挖掘美国总统的剧本，埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 表示金钱可能变得毫无意义，并推测宇宙是外星人创造的计算机模拟，而且 - 说到这里 - 英联邦运动会在格拉斯哥开幕，查尔斯国王走出了神秘博士的塔迪斯。澳大利亚 奥运会开幕 |英联邦运动会在格拉斯哥开幕，由于维多利亚迟迟退出，主办方被迫缩减了赛事规模。关注开幕式现场直播，而英联邦最快的两轮男子马特·理查森(Matt Richardson)则否认了他有争议的转会英格兰队的影响，称其对他“零影响”。劳动力“过敏” |直言不讳的工党后座议员埃德·胡西克声称，该党已经对异议和争议变得“过敏”，并警告说，在严格控制的全国会议上表现出的团结可能会适得其反。鸟鸣 |七岁的迈尔斯·菲利普斯 (Miles Phillips) 创作的关于东部罗塞拉花的歌曲在网上疯传，人们将其与 Strokes、B-52 和泰勒·斯威夫特 (Taylor Swift) 进行比较。他告诉 Rafqa Touma 他是如何写的。起源黑客 |该公司证实，Origin Energy 客户的地址、电话号码和部分银行账户数据已被黑客入侵。南辕北辙|北领地一位部长因声称需要利用该地区的地下水来防止地球磁极翻转而受到批评，该说法在科学上不准确。世界马斯克的沉思|埃隆·马斯克承认他成为了
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
